params.text:
Caring for dentures

Many of the people you support wear full or partial dentures, and these need daily care just as natural teeth do. Dentures collect food, plaque and bacteria, and a neglected denture can cause bad breath, sore gums and infection. Clean dentures also help a person eat and speak comfortably and feel confident.

  • Clean daily: remove and clean dentures every day, and rinse them after meals.
  • Out at night: take dentures out overnight to rest and protect the gums.
  • Named and safe: store them in a clean, labelled container so they are not lost or mixed up.

Handle dentures with care — they are easily dropped and broken, and a lost or damaged denture can leave a person unable to eat properly for some time.

Cleaning and overnight storage

To clean dentures, remove them and brush them over a bowl of water or a soft towel, so they are not damaged if they slip from your hands. Use a denture brush with soap and water or a non-abrasive denture cleaner — ordinary toothpaste is too abrasive and can scratch and wear the denture. Rinse them well, and also clean the person's gums, tongue and any remaining natural teeth with a soft brush before the dentures go back in.

Dentures should be taken out at night. Leaving them out gives the gums a rest and helps prevent a common infection of the mouth called denture stomatitis, a soreness under the plate. Store them overnight in a clean, labelled pot — in water or a denture-soaking solution, or clean and dry, following the manufacturer's and the person's plan. NICE guidance recommends marking dentures with the person's name, which is a great help in a shared setting. If dentures are loose, cracked or causing soreness, report it and arrange a dental review.
